data.title: Economic ‘pragmatism’ powers Indonesians’ positive view of China, survey finds
data.description:
China has emerged as a broadly positive partner for Indonesia in the public mind, according to a new survey, driven by visible economic engagement and investment on the ground, even as public support stops short of seeing Beijing as a full strategic partner.
The 2025 China–Indonesia Survey, released on December 18 by Jakarta-based think tank Centre of Economic and Law Studies (Celios), suggests Indonesians increasingly view ties with Beijing through a transactional lens, welcoming economic and technological cooperation while remaining cautious in other areas.
Overall, 66 per cent of the 1,022 respondents surveyed described relations between Jakarta and Beijing as positive, particularly in the areas of economic and technological cooperation.
President Prabowo Subianto was seen as “close” to Beijing by 94 per cent of respondents, a relationship they said could generate economic opportunities, strengthen Indonesia’s diplomacy and help balance US influence.
Mainland China was Indonesia’s third-largest foreign investor with US$5.4 billion in realised investment between January and September this year, behind Singapore’s US$12.6 billion and Hong Kong’s US$7.3 billion, according to the Indonesia Investment Coordinating Board.

Most respondents, or 78 per cent, supported Prabowo’s plan to extend the China-funded Jakarta-Bandung high-speed railway to Surabaya, citing transport modernisation, improved connectivity, and supporting economic growth as reasons behind their support.
China is also the favourite destination for overseas study for 31 per cent of respondents, beating Australia, Britain, the European Union and the United States.
When it comes to trade, only 32 per cent of respondents preferred China to be Indonesia’s top partner, while 22 per cent chose Western states and 17 per cent opted for Southeast Asian neighbours.
“Overall, the survey mirrors public hope that the Indonesia-China relationship can yield positive results,” Muhammad Zulfikar Rakhmat, director of the China-Indonesia desk at Celios, told This Week in Asia.
According to Muhammad, the positive public perception of China is due to its “visible” investment activities in Indonesia.
“In general, the relations [between Indonesia and China] have been transactional and conditional,” he said.
“In the future, as long as [China] delivers, the relationship will go further. The Indonesian people assume that there are many benefits that can be obtained from this relationship because there is tangibility; the people can see [China-funded] projects.”
Yeta Purnama, a Celios researcher focusing on China-Indonesia relations, said Beijing’s “soft diplomacy” had helped shape the public’s view of bilateral ties.
“[China] is very active, especially in the education sector. There have been many collaborations, visits, and other activities taking place at various campuses in Indonesia. In addition, sister city cooperation in various regions is also carried out very actively. This form of soft diplomacy appears to be quite successful in influencing public perception,” she said.


Lagging in defence
The survey underlines that “pragmatism” is the foundation of the Indonesian public’s positive views of China, especially on economic cooperation, according to Yeremia Lalisang, an international relations lecturer at the University of Indonesia.
“When asked about their preferred primary partner, only around 30 per cent of respondents named China. Pragmatism doesn’t really translate into a perception that China is our main trade partner. The [survey] indicates that respondents support partner diversification in Indonesia’s foreign relations,” said Yeremia, who was not involved in the survey.
China has established itself as Indonesia’s biggest trade partner, with bilateral trade reaching US$135.17 billion in 2024.
In defence, 42 per cent of respondents voted Russia as the top source of defence and military equipment, followed by China’s 19 per cent, and the US with 13 per cent.
“This shows that the comprehensive strategic partnership between Indonesia and China has not yet gone beyond economic partnership,” Yeremia said.
Respondents might also have been influenced by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and its role in the civil war in Syria, Yeta said.
“For Russia, war is seen as a showcase of military power. China has also seen its military strength increase in recent years. Meanwhile, the involvement of Western countries in financing arms for Israel has influenced public perception in Indonesia, which is generally on the side of Palestine,” Yeta said.

Points of friction
While Jakarta generally enjoys smooth relations with Beijing, analysts point out that some areas of friction remain, particularly in sovereignty issues.
The survey found that the 34 per cent of respondents who viewed China negatively were driven by concerns relating to maritime boundaries in the South China Sea, lack of trust, negative media coverage of China, and human rights issues.
Indonesia is not a claimant in the South China Sea territorial dispute, but part of its resource-rich North Natuna Sea falls within China’s so-called nine-dash lines, under which Beijing lays claim to most of the disputed waterway.
Recent negative media coverage has also centred on the existence of an airport inside a nickel hub – dotted with Chinese companies – in Central Sulawesi. The airport was accused of operating international flights without proper authorisation and dubbed by officials as “a state within a state”.
Yeta said the Indonesian public was also concerned about Chinese exports flooding local markets and potentially threatening the country’s businesses, including big manufacturing companies.
Despite the friction, Jakarta and Beijing are expected to forge closer ties next year, particularly in trade and investment, even as analysts urge Prabowo to keep diversifying export markets and boost ties with other nations.
“China will always be part of our calculations. In the future, it is difficult to imagine Indonesia’s foreign relations moving significantly away from China, but China can never be the only partner,” Yeremia said.

data.title: Judges slam lawyers, quash convictions of 11 Hong Kong villagers in ‘ding’ case
data.description:
A Hong Kong appellate court has quashed the convictions of 11 men who were found guilty of conspiring to fraudulently obtain building licences from the government more than a decade ago, finding that they did not receive a fair trial because of their lawyers’ incompetence.
In a written judgment delivered on Tuesday, the Court of Appeal ruled that the appellants’ lawyers failed to act in their clients’ best interests by adopting a defence strategy that best suited a developer prosecuted in the same case and a “wider circle” that was concerned with the outcome of the landmark trial in 2015.
“We feel compelled to find [the appellants] have been deprived of a fair trial in what is arguably the worst case of conflict that this court, in its collective experience, has been asked to consider,” the three presiding judges said in their verdict.
The appeal arose from the first criminal trial implicating those involved in the sales and purchases of the so-called ding right, which allows male indigenous villagers to seek permission to build three-storey homes in the New Territories.
In 2015, the District Court handed down jail sentences of up to three years to developer David Lee Yam-pui and 11 men from Sha Tin’s Tai Che village for conspiring to deceive the government into believing the villagers intended to build houses for their own occupation between 2008 and 2011.
While Lee appeared to have sold certain plots to the 11 residents, he had covertly acquired their ding right and had them forfeit their interests in the land once they successfully applied for a building licence from the Lands Department.
Lee’s family dropped his appeal after he died in 2022.
Defence counsel Philip Wong Chi-wai, who represented all 12 accused in the trial, advanced what he called a “broad and general defence” – that all the villagers involved made true declarations to the department when applying for a licence, and it was impossible to infer dishonesty on their part.
Justices Carlye Chu Fun-ling, Derek Pang Wai-cheong and Anthea Pang Po-kam found that Wong failed to consider alternative strategies for the 11 villagers and properly advise them to testify in court regarding what they believed was the legality of their actions.
The appeal proceedings revealed that Wong’s representation was the result of a discussion between his instructing lawyer, Jim Yiu-ming, and the Heung Yee Kuk, the rural powerhouse representing the interests of landlords.
The appellants’ legal fees were settled by an anonymous person who happened to have redeemed the mortgage that Lee took out for the development project in the village.

None of the 11 could contact their legal representatives without the help of a middleman identified as Cheng Ching-on, who also had a financial interest in the scheme.
The appellants said they were told to sign documents purporting to endorse certain legal instructions without knowing what they were. Some also complained they had never met the lawyers individually and were discouraged from giving evidence in the trial.
The judges said the events were disturbing and made one wonder if the “atrocious” idea was true that the original defence team intended to dissuade the 11 appellants from taking the witness box because what they said might harm Lee’s case.
The court found it impossible to dismiss the complaint of conflict of interest against Wong and Jim, even as it brushed aside the appellants’ constitutional challenge to the rural housing policy.
In 2021, the city’s top court upheld male indigenous villagers’ right to apply to build three-storey houses on their ancestral or government-granted land, although the department still retains the discretion to grant or dismiss an application.
The Court of Appeal ruled on Tuesday that the government’s requirement for applicants to declare their eligibility under the policy was justified to prevent abuse of the mechanism.
Prosecutors have not indicated whether they will seek a retrial for the 11 appellants.

data.title: ‘Saturation attacks’: drone swarms key for wartime victory, Chinese military outlet says
data.description:
The key to achieving wartime victory through expendable drones lies in “saturation attacks based on numerical superiority”, according to the Chinese military’s mouthpiece as the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) accelerates its development of unmanned systems.
The PLA Daily published a commentary on Tuesday titled “Exploring the Winning Formula for Unmanned Attrition Warfare” written by Zhou Xiaoli, Zhang Changfang and Zhu Qichao, although the outlet did not specify their institutional affiliations.
The authors said that in future warfare, it would be imperative to “flexibly deploy low-cost unmanned systems to continuously deplete the opponent’s high-value combat assets”, thereby “achieving optimal combat effectiveness with minimal battlefield expenditure”.


The article said that by releasing large numbers of drones in concentrated formations, unmanned systems could “launch simultaneous operations across multiple domains and directions”, thereby “forcing the enemy into a state of exhaustion and passive defence”.
Furthermore, unmanned combat forces could coordinate across multiple battlefield sectors, the article said. For instance, ground unmanned combat vehicles and aerial drones could operate in tandem, with “the former responsible for firepower strikes and target guidance, while the latter handles reconnaissance and support”.
And in wartime practice, unmanned combat systems could induce exhaustion in adversaries through “routine, incremental and sustained operations”.
Moreover, leveraging the flexibility, intelligence and diversity of unmanned systems could better achieve “asymmetric victory”, according to the article.
To improve the effectiveness of unmanned combat, such systems required greater autonomy, coordination and intelligence while also “integrating traditional tactics with unmanned technology”.
The PLA Daily article was published as Beijing assertively develops unmanned technology, viewing it as having a pivotal role in modern warfare and asymmetric combat as its operational capabilities are on display on the battlefield in Ukraine.
Drone operations are also regarded as a key factor in potential regional conflicts, including those in the Taiwan Strait region.
On December 11, the state news agency Xinhua reported that China’s massive aerial Jiu Tian drone carrier had completed its first flight, a debut that signalled the PLA’s potential swarming capacities, but the report did not mention when this occurred.
The Jiu Tian made its debut at China’s premier aviation showcase, the Zhuhai air show, in November last year. It is reported to carry up to 100 cruise weapons or small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), including suicide drones. These armaments can be deployed simultaneously from both sides of the fuselage, significantly extending its operational range.
It can also carry a range of payloads on eight hardpoints and is capable of intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance assignments, as well as electronic warfare. Military commentators have noted that these characteristics will give the Jiu Tian exceptional saturation strike capabilities.
Days later, state broadcaster CCTV reported that China’s latest long-endurance stealth UAV, the CH-7, had also completed its maiden flight.
The drone is capable of long endurance, stealth and high cruising speed, ground reconnaissance, data support and communications under complex conditions, according to CCTV.
In September, China showcased multiple new unmanned systems during its Victory Day parade, including loyal wingman drones, collaborative combat aircraft and unmanned combat vehicles.


Meanwhile, China’s primary geopolitical rival, the United States, is also seeking to establish its advantage on the unmanned battlefield.
In June, US President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders aimed at bolstering the unmanned capabilities of the world’s most powerful military.
US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth subsequently called for more proactive action in July, urging the US military to aggressively procure small UAVs and related components, with the goal of widespread deployment of these systems by late next year.

data.title: Russia ‘strikes Ukraine before Christmas’ with over 650 drones, dozens of missiles
data.description:
Russia fired more than 650 drones and three dozen missiles at Ukraine in a large-scale attack that began during the night and stretched into daylight hours Tuesday, officials said. At least three people were killed, including a 4-year-old child.
The barrage struck homes and the power grid in 13 regions of Ukraine, causing widespread outages in bitter temperatures, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said, a day after he described recent progress on finding a peace deal “quite solid”.
The attack “is an extremely clear signal of Russian priorities” Zelensky said on the Telegram messaging app.
“A strike before Christmas, when people want to be with their families, at home, in safety. A strike, in fact, in the midst of negotiations that are being conducted to end this war. Putin cannot accept the fact that we must stop killing.”


Ukrainian and European officials have complained that Russian President Vladimir Putin is not sincerely engaging with US-led peace efforts.
US President Donald Trump has for months been pressing for a peace agreement, but the negotiations have become entangled in the very different demands from Moscow and Kyiv.
US envoy Steve Witkoff said on Sunday he held “productive and constructive” talks in Florida with Ukrainian and European representatives. Trump was less effusive on Monday, saying, “The talks are going along.”
Initial reports from Ukrainian emergency services said the 4-year-old child died in Ukraine’s northwestern Zhytomyr region, while a drone killed a woman in the Kyiv region, and another civilian death was recorded in the western Khmelnytskyi region, according to Volodymyr Zelensky.

Authorities in the western regions of Rivne, Ternopil and Lviv, as well as the northern Sumy region, reported damage to energy infrastructure or power outages after the attack.
In the southern Odesa region, Russia struck energy, port, transport, industrial and residential infrastructure, according to regional head Oleh Kiper.
A merchant ship and over 120 homes were damaged, he said.
Russia’s latest attacks once again focused primarily on Ukraine’s energy infrastructure.
The situation for many residents has deteriorated due to freezing temperatures, with electricity and heating disruptions compounding existing hardships.
Moscow has intensified strikes during the winter months in an apparent effort to increase pressure on Ukraine to accept Russian terms in ongoing talks aimed at ending the war.
Additional reporting by dpa

data.title: Hong Kong tram driver gets 4 weeks in jail for crash that killed girl, 3
data.description:
A Hong Kong court handed down a four-week jail sentence to a tram driver earlier convicted for careless driving that killed a three-year-old girl and injured three others, with the punishment significantly reduced due to the accident being deemed “inevitable”.
Deputy Magistrate Chung Wing-sze of Eastern Court on Tuesday cited a probation officer’s report that 50-year-old Li Yaodong was genuinely remorseful and had accepted legal responsibility.
Chung also said the defendant’s colleagues had petitioned for leniency, praising him as “dedicated to his work” and pointing to him twice winning the “best driver” award during his five years of service.
The accident took place outside Kennedy Town Swimming Pool on the morning of August 15, 2024. Li’s tram struck the girl, her six-year-old sister, their grandfather and the family’s helper as they were crossing Des Voeux Road West.
The girl was found unconscious under the tram and later pronounced dead in hospital.
Evidence showed that the grandfather carried the girl with one arm and held the hand of her sister as they waited at a traffic island. Security footage indicated that they took a step forward despite a red light being on.
While Li said he had already stopped the vehicle after realising that the tram had struck the pedestrians, Chung found the driver did not pay enough attention to road conditions on a rainy day and did not brake in time.
“But considering the uniqueness of each case, a tram cannot swerve, and it takes time to brake the vehicle. Therefore, the collision was inevitable,” she said. “These factors will significantly reduce the sentence.”
The magistrate said she had taken into consideration mitigation letters from the defendant’s colleagues at Hong Kong Tramways, where he worked from 2019 to 2024 after moving to the city from mainland China.
Chung added that Li’s colleagues had signed a petition letter to plead for a lenient sentence.
The court earlier heard from an expert’s report that the collision occurred in a split second, giving the defendant only 0.9 seconds to react.
Driving records showed that the tram continued moving for another 12.7 metres (41.7 feet) after the impact, indicating that Li failed to stop in time.
The report concluded that the accident was inevitable as the driver “may or may not have been able” to brake in time.
Chung sentenced Li to four weeks in prison after taking into consideration the case’s mitigating factors.
Defence lawyer Leo Yu indicated that Li would appeal and applied for the defendant’s bail.
The court granted Li a HK$10,000 (US$1,285) bail but barred him from leaving the city during the proceedings and told him to report regularly to his local police station.
Chung also ordered that the defendant be released pending appeal proceedings, noting that he had already been remanded in custody for two weeks.

data.title: Drones meet dancers in Hong Kong performance of Lucie in the Sky
data.description:
Lucie in the Sky is an award-winning show from Australia that was presented by Hong Kong’s East Kowloon Cultural Centre (EKCC) as part of its grand opening season.
In keeping with the centre’s mission to pioneer the creative use of technology in the arts, the piece by the Australasian Dance Collective (ADC) features six human dancers interacting with five drones.
The tiny drones – seen by the audience as lights floating in the air – are beautifully choreographed and interact elegantly with the dancers.
The piece is undoubtedly impressive, combining technology with dance. The dance itself, on the other hand, is disappointing – the concept may be imaginative, but the choreography is not.

Under its former identity as the Expressions Dance Company, ADC has long-time connections with Hong Kong: its founding artistic director, Maggie Sietsma, was dean of dance at the Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts, and her successor, Natalie Weir, has created a number of works for Hong Kong companies.
Lucie in the Sky was created in 2023 by the company’s current artistic director, Amy Hollingsworth, in collaboration with Verity Studios, which has provided drones for shows by acts including Cirque du Soleil and Celine Dion.
Hollingsworth is a helicopter pilot as well as a choreographer, so flying machines, albeit small ones in this case, are no problem for her. Her stated aim with the production is to explore how we coexist with technology and “whether we can design a future that honours both humanity and innovation”.
According to the house programme, each cast member – human or drone – has a name and a set of characteristics: for example, one female dancer is The Caregiver, protective/compassionate/thoughtful; one of the drones is Skip – The Jester, playful/charismatic/cheeky/irrepressible. In practice, it is hard to identify which character, human or drone, is which.

There is a succession of scenes in which individual dancers interact with individual drones, punctuated by moments where they come together as a group, freezing into tableaux enhanced by striking lighting effects. But the choreography lacks continuity and coherence and there is relatively little actual dance – the male dancers produce some athletic tumbling moves and barrel jumps, but even these are quite disconnected sequences of steps.
One of the more intriguing encounters, between a female dancer and the blue drone (Rue – The Sage, melancholic/wise/contemplative), appears to be about grief and loss. Another features the purple drone (Lucie – The Friend), which did indeed appear to be making friends with another female dancer.
However, the piece ends with the same pair apparently in a very different position – the human girl cowers on the ground when Lucie approaches her and at the end lies flat on her back, apparently dead, while Lucie sails serenely over her.
The concept of anthropomorphising drones is dubious – people whose privacy is invaded by the use of drones, or those living in war zones where drones rain down death and destruction every day, are unlikely to see them as cute technological companions.
Perhaps in reference to this is a scene that is suggestive of the use of drones in warfare: a dancer runs around in terror, looking up at the sky.

Ultimately, it is not clear what we are supposed to take from all this. Is Hollingsworth saying that technology can be our friend, but also a source of danger? If so, this is certainly true, but scarcely a revelation.
In any case, these particular drones are innocently entertaining – in fact, the floating lights interacting with people are irresistibly reminiscent of Tinkerbell in Peter Pan – and the way they join the humans for the curtain call was particularly charming.
“Australasian Dance Collective: Lucie in the Sky”, The Hall, East Kowloon Cultural Centre. Reviewed: December 20.

data.title: Who is Ben Gorham, who’s been hanging out with Kendall Jenner? Meet the ex-basketballer and founder of luxury fragrance brand Byredo
data.description:
Kendall Jenner may be ending 2025 with a possible new beau. Following her split from rapper Bad Bunny in September 2024, she has been courting romance rumours with fragrance tycoon Ben Gorham, 18 years her senior. The pair stepped out in Los Angeles recently for a shopping spree at La Maison Francaise Antiques, followed by a coffee run at Architecture Bookstore Café, reports Page Six.
This isn’t the first time the two have been linked. In September, Jenner and Gorham were seen in West Hollywood looking at books. On another occasion that same month, they grabbed lunch at Summerland’s Field + Fort cafe. The following month, they reportedly had a Parisian outing, per the Daily Mail.

It is unclear whether romance is blooming between the model and the Byredo founder, or if a collaboration is in the works. He supposedly has a wife, Natasa, with whom he shares two daughters, Inez and Anouk, according to The Cut.
Here’s everything to know about Ben Gorham.
What’s his background?

Ben Gorham was born to an Indian mother and a half-Scottish, half-French Canadian father in Sweden, per beauty website Into the Gloss. He has a sister, per The Guardian, and they were brought up by his mother. “She grew up in a garage in Mumbai with five brothers and was extremely poor,” Gorham said of his mother.
The family moved to Toronto when he was 11, but he completed high school in New York and moved back to Toronto to study business at Ryerson University. He eventually switched to studying politics and interior design before getting a fine arts degree from Stockholm Art School.
He’s a former basketball player

Gorham found his passion for basketball at a very young age. He continued playing after finishing high school and eventually became a Division One athlete in Canada. He went pro, playing basketball in Italy, Germany and Sweden, but quit due to immigration issues, per The Guardian.
How did he get into perfumery?

After graduating from Stockholm Art School, Gorham crossed paths with perfumer Pierre Wulff and was inspired to explore the world of scents. “It’s almost as if I’ve had two lives,” he shared in an interview with SCMP, speaking of his transition from sports to fragrance. Gorham started his perfume journey by making scented candles at home and later founded Byredo, he told Into the Gloss.

The first perfume he created was meant to capture the essence of his father, who left his family when he was young.
Byredo eventually expanded into home fragrances, beauty, fashion and jewellery. In 2022, Gorham sold the majority stake of the company to Spanish luxury group Puig for a reported price of US$1.18 billion.

data.title: Hong Kong customs arrests 5 in suspected HK$8.9 billion ‘ghost trading’ racket
data.description:
Hong Kong customs has arrested five people on suspicion of laundering HK$8.9 billion (US$1.1 billion) in suspected crime proceeds over a four-year period, the first time authorities have uncovered a syndicate using sophisticated fake shipping websites to facilitate what investigators called “ghost trading”.
Senior investigator Tsoi Kim-fai of customs’ financial investigation bureau said on Tuesday that the operation last week led to the arrest of two men and three women, aged 28 to 59, who allegedly operated 29 bank accounts to process over 3,700 suspicious transactions between January 2022 and this month.
Tsoi explained that “ghost trading” referred to trade that did not exist in reality, with no actual imports or exports of goods. Such exchanges relied on falsified documents, such as fake contracts and invoices, to create bogus transaction records.
Using these records, criminals could disguise illicit money as legitimate trade income, allowing them to transfer large amounts of money while concealing the true source of the funds, he said.
“This is the first time customs has discovered a money laundering syndicate using elaborately designed fake websites to complement their falsified trade documents. This was done to disguise ‘ghost trading’ and the underlying money laundering activities,” Tsoi said.
“The amount involved is massive, and the methods used are more complex than in previous cases, significantly increasing the difficulty of the investigation.”
Tsoi said the money laundering syndicate created sophisticated, fake shipping company websites and falsified trade documents to build an illusion that trading activities were genuinely taking place, thereby deceiving banks when they conducted due diligence checks with customers.
“Taking one instance of fake trade from the case as an example, the syndicate claimed to the bank that its company had shipped goods equivalent to about HK$86 million and, consequently, its corporate account received the corresponding payment,” Tsoi said.
“If bank employees used the information provided on the syndicate’s ocean bills of lading to check these fraudulent websites, they would find fabricated shipping status updates that appeared to match the documents.”
During their investigation, customs officers identified three local suspects – core members of the syndicate – who allegedly used accounts under their companies’ names to process large sums of money from unknown sources, originating from overseas firms in the Middle East and Africa.
The funds were then rapidly funnelled to licensed currency exchanges, local and mainland firms and private individual accounts.
The two arrested men also used their personal accounts to handle funds of unknown origin, while three of the suspects opened a total of 15 corporate and 14 personal accounts across 14 local banks, and received over 3,700 transfers of funds from unknown sources totalling HK$8.9 billion over four years, Tsoi said.
“The three companies involved had no import or export declaration records, and the registered addresses of the companies were either personal residential or business addresses of other companies,” he explained.
Customs later identified another two local women who had significant financial transactions with core members of the syndicate and allegedly assisted in the handling of suspected crime proceeds.

Customs officers arrested the five people last Thursday and Friday on suspicion of money laundering while searching their residences and offices in North Point, Tseung Kwan O and Prince Edward, seizing mobile phones, bank cards and falsified trade documents.
Tsoi said the five individuals had been released on bail pending further investigation, and assets under their names amounting to about HK$55 million had been frozen. Further arrests had not been ruled out.
The senior investigator said customs would continue to look into the backgrounds of the suspects, the sources of funds and the flow of capital, while conducting in-depth probes into the relevant fake websites.
In Hong Kong, money laundering offences carry a maximum penalty of 14 years’ imprisonment and a fine of HK$5 million.

data.title: Hong Kong Red Cross unveils HK$420 million relief fund for Tai Po fire victims
data.description:
Hong Kong Red Cross has raised HK$420 million (US$54 million) to support victims of the deadly Tai Po blaze, launching a three to five year relief plan covering the financial, mental health and medical needs of those affected.
Almost a month after the fire at Wang Fuk Court – which killed at least 161 people, injured dozens and displaced nearly 5,000 – the NGO said on Tuesday that it aimed not only to help victims rebuild their lives, but also to assist the whole of Hong Kong in recovering from the tragedy.
Bonnie So Yuen-han, CEO of the Hong Kong Red Cross, said the organisation had raised a total of HK$420 million and would allocate HK$240 million for direct cash assistance.
“We would initiate a community recovery programme that runs for three to five years, including cash assistance for more vulnerable families, as well as medical consultations and psychological support,” she said.
“We would also strengthen the disaster preparedness of Hong Kong people through training on mental health support and first aid, as well as organising support groups to foster mutual support in the community.”
Hong Kong Red Cross is among numerous NGOs and charitable groups – alongside government efforts and public donations – providing immediate relief and support to victims of the fire.
So said the organisation would conduct an assessment of families’ needs to ensure more targeted assistance.
For immediate relief, each household will receive a pre-loaded Octopus card worth HK$6,000 from the social worker assigned to the family by January. In the next three to six months, households will also receive monthly cash assistance ranging from HK$4,000 to HK$15,000, depending on size and needs.

Foreign domestic helpers will receive a pre-loaded Octopus card with HK$3,000 in credit and HK$5,000 in cash. For helpers who died in the blaze, their families will be provided with HK$60,000 in cash assistance over 12 months. The NGO’s Philippine and Indonesian branches have also assisted in repatriating the bodies.
The remaining HK$120 million will be reserved for long-term support and recovery, dedicated to around 1,000 families estimated to require additional aid.
So said the organisation had spoken to 151 victims while distributing cash assistance and found that more than 60 per cent reported some degree of emotional distress, such as sleep problems, underscoring the need for sustained mental health support.
Mobile clinics have been set up to provide consultations, health advice and physiotherapy to residents and domestic helpers.
The NGO’s mental health professionals have also offered services to firefighters, paramedics and the general public through a round-the-clock support hotline, which will operate until January 24.
To promote long-term community recovery, the organisation will set up service points offering comprehensive support such as first aid, psychological counselling, community health advice and disease management.
So added that all administrative expenses would not exceed 5 per cent of the total donations, and an independent financial audit report would be submitted afterwards.

data.title: China school awards top and hardworking pupils with merit certificates imprinted bedding sets
data.description:
A secondary school in China has awarded stellar pupils with tailor-made quilt covers, bedsheets and blankets.
The move has won praise from internet users for understanding juvenile psychology.
The decision to shun traditional red paper-made merit certificates was taken by Yucai Dachengbei School in Hangzhou, Zhejiang province, eastern China.

Instead, it awarded its pupils with various bedclothes which bear the names of individual pupils and their specific award titles, Chao News reported.
In mid-December, the school held an event to award pupils who performed well at a series of key tests in November.
The pupils, whose total scores over a range of subjects were ranked in the top five in their grade, received a quilt cover.

Those with a score from a single subject test in the top five positions received a bedsheet.
Pupils who had made great progress in their total scores were awarded a pillow, while their counterparts who made remarkable progress in a single subject received a blanket as a prize.
“This award is custom-made for you,” a teacher said at the award presentation ceremony.
“I hope our effort in preparing these awards will enhance your confidence and courage. Keep on studying hard. We are looking forward to your future success.”

No quota limitation was set for pupils to receive awards and as a result some received numerous items of bedding.
According to Jiang Yan, a teacher at the school, the special awards were designed by the teachers themselves.
“We think the kids will be happy when sleeping in the ‘merit certificate piles’. Their happiness will be doubled because every award is different and tailor-made for them,” Jiang told the media.
Some grandparents of the pupils smiled when they used the bedclothes because they were sleeping in “honorary quilts” won by their grandchildren.
Parents also commended the award creation which was described as “practical and meaningful”.
“I cannot wait to use it instantly without washing it, ha,” one parent said online.
The story sparked a passionate discussion on mainland social media.

“These specially made items allow the efforts of students to be easily visible to their family. It results in a twofold motivational effect,” said one internet user.
Another person praised the school: “This school understands the psychology of pupils well. The pupils are delighted and cheered while their parents are proud of them.”
Young students in China often face acute academic pressure.
Schools usually hold two crucial tests, in the middle and at the end of each semester, and rank pupils according to their scores.
Most schools award students with red paper-made merit certificates and study-related goods like pens and exercise books.
